toolUseResult.originalFile:
# rockybot — project notes for Claude

## Project Overview

rockybot is a self-hosted autonomous research assistant. It watches an Obsidian vault and spawns Claude Code CLI (`--print` mode) for five kinds of research tasks. Three Docker services make up the stack:

| Service | Image | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `rockybot` | `ghcr.io/mrwong/rockybot` | Core bot — polls vault, runs all watchers |
| `rockybot-bridge` | `ghcr.io/mrwong/rockybot-bridge` | Watches vault for changes, rebuilds Quartz static site |
| `rockybot-headless` | `ghcr.io/mrwong/rockybot-headless` | Obsidian Sync daemon (remote deployments only) |

The bot is deployed in the homelab via `~/src/boughtmyowncloud` — that repo owns the `docker-compose.yml` entry, secrets injection, and GitOps pipeline. To redeploy after changing rockybot code: push a release tag here (triggers GHCR image publish), then push to `main` in boughtmyowncloud (triggers GitOps sync).

## Repository Layout

```
services/
  bot/
    src/
      index.js            — main poll loop (global mutex, runs all watchers sequentially)
      *-watcher.js        — five watcher modules (see below)
      claude-runner.js    — spawns `claude --print` subprocess; subscription → API key fallback; interactive auth flow
      notifier.js         — Discord webhook + Twilio WhatsApp alerts
      discord-bot.js      — discord.js WebSocket bot for interactive auth prompts (optional)
      login-runner.js     — spawns `claude login` headlessly to capture OAuth URL
      seeder.js           — seeds vault with scaffold files on first boot
    package.json          — canonical version source
  obsidian-bridge/        — chokidar watcher → Quartz rebuild
  obsidian-headless/      — `ob sync` daemon
vault-scaffold/           — default vault files baked into bot Docker image
templates/                — Obsidian templates seeded to vault on startup
tests/
  unit/                   — fast, no Claude required
  integration/            — spawns real Claude; use sparingly
scripts/
  release.sh              — semver bump + CHANGELOG + tag
docs/                     — INSTALLATION, WORKFLOW, CONFIGURATION, DEVELOPMENT guides
docker-compose.yml        — production stack
docker-compose.dev.yml    — dev stack (single container, temp vault, haiku models, $0.10 caps)
```

## The Five Watchers

All watchers follow the same pattern: scan vault → detect trigger → run `claude --print` with a prompt → update vault.

| Watcher | Trigger | What Claude does | Default budget | Default model |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **inbox** | `research/inbox/*.md` with `status: pending` | Full multi-page research, writes topic dir, updates `research/index.md`, Karpathy backlink pass | `$2.00` | `sonnet` |
| **amend** | `> [!claude]` callout in any page | Targeted inline edit per callout instruction | `$0.50` | `haiku` |
| **expand** | `> [!expand]` callout | Creates new sub-page on callout topic, replaces callout with wikilink | `$1.00` | `sonnet` |
| **revise** | `> [!revise]` callout in topic `index.md` | Corpus-level rewrite — finds all wikilinked sub-pages, revises each in-place | `$4.00` | `sonnet` |
| **lint** | `status: pending` in `research/lint-trigger.md` | Full wiki audit: orphaned pages, broken wikilinks, writes `lint-report.md` | `$5.00` | `sonnet` |

Budgets and models are all overridable via env vars (`INBOX_BUDGET_USD`, `AMEND_MODEL`, etc.). See `docs/CONFIGURATION.md`.

## Key Architecture Notes

- `index.js` holds a **global mutex** — only one Claude subprocess runs at a time across all watchers.
- `claude-runner.js` tries the Claude subscription first; falls back to `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` if auth fails.
- Prompt files live in the vault (`research/*-prompt.md`) — user-editable, with hardcoded fallbacks if missing.
- The dev compose file (`docker-compose.dev.yml`) live-mounts `services/bot/src` so source changes take effect without rebuilding.
- Bot container mounts `~/.claude:/home/node/.claude` for Pro subscription billing auth.

## Development Workflow

```bash
# Run unit tests (no Claude required)
npm test

# Run integration tests (spawns real Claude — costs money)
npm run test:integration

# Start dev stack (isolated vault, haiku models, $0.10 caps)
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up

# Add a new watcher: follow the pattern in any existing *-watcher.js,
# register it in index.js, add budget/model env vars to .env.example,
# update docs/CONFIGURATION.md and docs/WORKFLOW.md
```

## Versioning policy

rockybot uses [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/): `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`.

| Bump | When |
|---|---|
| **major** | Breaking changes: removed or renamed env vars, incompatible config format changes, removed features |
| **minor** | New functionality: new watchers, new callout types, new env vars, new integrations |
| **patch** | Bug fixes, internal refactors, test improvements, CI changes, dependency updates |
| *(none)* | Documentation changes only (`docs:` commits, `.md` file edits) |

Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) prefixes in commit messages. The release script uses these to categorize and propose the right bump level:

- `feat:` → minor (or `feat!:` for major)
- `fix:` → patch (or `fix!:` for major)
- `refactor:`, `perf:` → patch, shown as "Changed"
- `test:`, `chore:` → patch, shown as "Internal"
- `docs:` → no version bump

## Releasing

```bash
./scripts/release.sh
```

The script:
1. Fetches latest tags from origin
2. Shows all commits since the last tag, grouped by type
3. Proposes a bump level based on commit prefixes
4. You confirm or type `major` / `minor` / `patch` to override
5. Confirms before making any changes
6. Writes a CHANGELOG.md entry, bumps `services/bot/package.json` and `services/obsidian-bridge/package.json`, commits as `chore: release vX.Y.Z`, and creates the git tag
7. Asks whether to push to origin (pushing the tag triggers GitHub Actions to publish Docker images)

**Never bump the version manually** — always go through `./scripts/release.sh` so the CHANGELOG stays in sync.

## What lives where

| Canonical version | `services/bot/package.json` |
|---|---|
| Kept in sync | `services/obsidian-bridge/package.json` |
| Release history | `CHANGELOG.md` |
| Docker image tags | Driven by git tags via GitHub Actions |

## Tests

Every bug fix and new feature must include tests. See `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` for how to run them.

+- `claude-runner.js` tries the Claude subscription first; on auth failure it either enters the interactive Discord flow (if `DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH=true`) or falls back to `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` automatically.

- The dev compose file (`docker-compose.dev.yml`) live-mounts `services/bot/src` so source changes take effect without rebuilding.
- Bot container mounts `~/.claude:/home/node/.claude` for Pro subscription billing auth.

input.old_string:
### Discord

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL` | Webhook URL for a Discord channel. rockybot posts an embed on each watcher completion and on Claude auth expiry. See [NOTIFICATIONS.md](NOTIFICATIONS.md) for setup. |
input.new_string:
### Discord

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL` | Webhook URL for a Discord channel. rockybot posts an embed on each watcher completion and on Claude auth expiry. See [NOTIFICATIONS.md](NOTIFICATIONS.md) for setup. |

### Interactive Discord auth (optional)

When `DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH=true` and subscription auth fails, the bot pauses the failing task, spawns `claude login` headlessly to obtain an OAuth URL, and posts it to Discord as a clickable button. You can re-authorize Claude from your phone or browser without SSH-ing into the host. After the timeout the task auto-falls back to the API key.

Requires a Discord **bot token** (not just a webhook) and the bot to be added to your server. See [NOTIFICATIONS.md](NOTIFICATIONS.md#interactive-discord-auth) for setup.

| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH` | `false` | Set to `true` to enable. Requires `DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN` and `DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID`. Only meaningful when `CLAUDE_SUBSCRIPTION_MODE=true`. |
| `DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN` | — | Discord bot token from the Developer Portal. |
| `DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID` | — | ID of the channel where auth prompts are posted. |
| `DISCORD_AUTH_TIMEOUT_MINUTES` | `5` | Minutes to wait for a user response before auto-falling back to the API key. |

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Configuration

All configuration is via environment variables. Copy `.env.example` to `.env` and adjust.

## Required

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | Anthropic API key for pay-per-use billing. Required unless using subscription mode exclusively and you never hit rate limits. Get one at [console.anthropic.com](https://console.anthropic.com/). |

## Paths

| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `VAULT_PATH` | `/vault` | Path inside the container where the Obsidian vault is mounted. Must match your volume mount. |

## Billing and auth

| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `CLAUDE_SUBSCRIPTION_MODE` | `false` | Set to `true` to use Claude Pro/Max subscription billing first, falling back to API key on auth errors or rate limits. Requires mounting `~/.claude` into the container. |

## Polling

| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `INBOX_POLL_SECONDS` | `600` | How often (in seconds) to check the vault for work. All five watchers run on this interval. 600s = 10 minutes. |

## Spending caps

Each cap limits how much Claude API spend a single watcher invocation can incur. The bot will stop the Claude process if the budget is exhausted mid-run.

| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `RESEARCH_BUDGET_USD` | `2.00` | Per research request (inbox watcher). New research tasks can be multi-page and use web search, so this is higher. |
| `AMEND_BUDGET_USD` | `0.50` | Per `[!claude]` callout file (amend watcher). These are targeted inline edits. |
| `EXPAND_BUDGET_USD` | `1.00` | Per `[!expand]` callout file (expand watcher). Creates a new sub-page. |
| `REVISE_BUDGET_USD` | `4.00` | Per `[!revise]` callout file (revise watcher). Revises all linked sub-pages, so budget is higher. |
| `LINT_BUDGET_USD` | `5.00` | Per lint pass (lint watcher). Audits the full research wiki, so budget is highest. |

Note: budget caps only apply when `CLAUDE_SUBSCRIPTION_MODE=false` or when falling back to API key billing.

## Model selection

| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `AMEND_MODEL` | `haiku` | Claude model for `[!claude]` amend tasks. Haiku is faster and cheaper for targeted edits. |
| `EXPAND_MODEL` | `sonnet` | Claude model for `[!expand]` tasks. Sonnet for better research quality. |
| `REVISE_MODEL` | `sonnet` | Claude model for `[!revise]` tasks. |
| `LINT_MODEL` | `sonnet` | Claude model for lint passes. |

The inbox watcher always uses `sonnet` (hardcoded) — new research tasks benefit from the full model capability.

Valid model values: `haiku`, `sonnet`, `opus`. Maps to the latest Claude model in each tier.

## Notifications

All notification variables are optional. If left blank, that notification channel is silently disabled.

### Discord

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL` | Webhook URL for a Discord channel. rockybot posts an embed on each watcher completion and on Claude auth expiry. See [NOTIFICATIONS.md](NOTIFICATIONS.md) for setup. |

### WhatsApp via Twilio

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID` | Twilio account SID |
| `TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN` | Twilio auth token |
| `TWILIO_FROM` | Sender number, e.g. `whatsapp:+14155238886` (Twilio sandbox) |
| `TWILIO_TO` | Your WhatsApp number, e.g. `whatsapp:+15551234567` |

All four Twilio vars must be set for WhatsApp notifications to send. Any missing var disables the whole channel.

## Development and testing

| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `DRY_RUN` | `false` | Set to `true` to run all watchers in dry-run mode: they detect pending files and log what they would do, but do not call Claude and do not modify any vault files. Useful for testing vault scanning without spending API budget. |

## Obsidian Bridge (full stack only)

| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `DOMAIN_NAME` | `localhost` | Domain used to configure the Quartz static site base URL. Set to your domain, e.g. `notes.example.com`. |
| `QUARTZ_OUTPUT` | `/quartz-output` | Path inside the bridge container where Quartz builds the static site. Must match the volume mount for notes-web. |

lines: | `DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL` | Webhook URL for a Discord channel. rockybot posts an embed on each watcher completion and on Claude auth expiry. See [NOTIFICATIONS.md](NOTIFICATIONS.md) for setup. |

+When `DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH=true` and subscription auth fails, the bot pauses the failing task, spawns `claude login` headlessly to obtain an OAuth URL, and posts it to Discord as a clickable button. You can re-authorize Claude from your phone or browser without SSH-ing into the host. After the timeout the task auto-falls back to the API key.
+Requires a Discord **bot token** (not just a webhook) and the bot to be added to your server. See [NOTIFICATIONS.md](NOTIFICATIONS.md#interactive-discord-auth) for setup.
+| `DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH` | `false` | Set to `true` to enable. Requires `DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN` and `DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID`. Only meaningful when `CLAUDE_SUBSCRIPTION_MODE=true`. |
+| `DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN` | — | Discord bot token from the Developer Portal. |
+| `DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID` | — | ID of the channel where auth prompts are posted. |
+| `DISCORD_AUTH_TIMEOUT_MINUTES` | `5` | Minutes to wait for a user response before auto-falling back to the API key. |

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Notifications

rockybot can send notifications when watchers complete (or fail) and when the Claude subscription session expires. Both channels are optional — if not configured, the bot runs silently.

## Discord

### Setup

1. Open the Discord server where you want notifications
2. Go to **Server Settings → Integrations → Webhooks → New Webhook**
3. Give it a name (e.g. "rockybot"), pick a channel, and copy the webhook URL
4. Add it to your `.env`:

```env
DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL=https://discord.com/api/webhooks/...
```

### What gets notified

- **Research complete** — when the inbox watcher finishes processing a request
- **Amend / expand / revise complete** — when a callout watcher finishes a file
- **Lint complete** — when a lint pass finishes
- **Error** — when any watcher fails (includes error message)
- **Claude auth expired** — when subscription mode fails with an auth error (throttled to max 2 alerts per day; bot automatically retries with API key)

### Embed format

Each notification is a Discord embed with:
- Title: watcher name + status (✅ or ❌)
- Description: file processed (for callout watchers) or topic (for inbox)
- Color: green for success, red for error

## WhatsApp via Twilio

### Setup

1. Create a [Twilio](https://www.twilio.com/) account
2. Enable the **WhatsApp Sandbox** in the Twilio console (Messaging → Try it Out → Send a WhatsApp message)
3. Follow the sandbox join instructions on your phone (send a join code to the sandbox number)
4. Add all four vars to your `.env`:

```env
TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID=ACxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN=your_auth_token
TWILIO_FROM=whatsapp:+14155238886
TWILIO_TO=whatsapp:+15551234567
```

`TWILIO_FROM` is the Twilio sandbox number (shown in the console). `TWILIO_TO` is your personal WhatsApp number in E.164 format with the `whatsapp:` prefix.

### What gets notified

Same events as Discord — WhatsApp and Discord fire for the same events. You can enable both, either, or neither.

### Moving to a production WhatsApp number

The sandbox requires re-joining every 72 hours. For persistent notifications, apply for a Twilio WhatsApp sender in the Twilio console. The environment variables are the same; only `TWILIO_FROM` changes to your approved sender number.

## Testing notifications

Set `DRY_RUN=true` and check that the bot starts without errors. Notifications only fire on real watcher completions, so to test a notification end-to-end: create a minimal research request in `research/inbox/` with `status: pending`, wait for the next poll cycle (or restart with a short `INBOX_POLL_SECONDS=10`), and verify the notification arrives.

lines: - **Amend / expand / revise complete** — when a callout watcher finishes a file
- **Lint complete** — when a lint pass finishes
- **Error** — when any watcher fails (includes error message)
-- **Claude auth expired** — when subscription mode fails with an auth error (throttled to max 2 alerts per day; bot automatically retries with API key)
+- **Claude auth expired** — when subscription mode fails with an auth error and `DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH=false` (throttled to max 2 alerts per day; bot automatically retries with API key)

input.new_string:
## Interactive Discord auth

When `DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH=true` and the Claude subscription session expires, instead of sending a passive alert and silently falling back to the API key, rockybot posts an interactive message to your Discord channel. The message includes a **clickable OAuth link** (generated by running `claude login` headlessly inside the container) and two buttons:

- **Authorize Claude** — opens the OAuth URL in your browser; click **Done** after authorizing
- **Use API Key** — immediately falls back to API key billing for the current task

After `DISCORD_AUTH_TIMEOUT_MINUTES` (default 5) the task auto-falls back to the API key.

This lets you re-auth Claude from your phone without SSH-ing into the Docker host.

### Setup

This requires a Discord **bot** (with a WebSocket connection to receive button clicks), not just a webhook.

1. Go to [discord.com/developers](https://discord.com/developers/applications) → **New Application**
2. Under **Bot**: click **Reset Token**, copy the token
3. Under **OAuth2 → URL Generator**: select scope `bot`, permissions **Send Messages** and **Read Message History**
4. Open the generated URL in your browser and add the bot to your server
5. In Discord, enable **Developer Mode** (User Settings → Advanced), then right-click your target channel → **Copy Channel ID**
6. Add to your `.env`:

```env
DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH=true
DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN=your-bot-token-here
DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID=REDACTED
DISCORD_AUTH_TIMEOUT_MINUTES=5   # optional, defaults to 5
```

The bot needs no privileged intents — button interactions don't require the Message Content intent.

> **Note:** `DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL` and `DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH` are independent. The webhook handles all regular notifications (task completions, errors). The bot token handles only the interactive auth prompt. You can run both simultaneously.

## WhatsApp via Twilio

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Development Guide

## Architecture overview

rockybot is a Node.js process that polls an Obsidian vault directory every N seconds. On each poll it runs five watchers in sequence, serialized behind a global mutex (only one Claude invocation at a time):

```
index.js (poll loop)
  └─ withGlobalLock('inbox',  scanInbox)      ← processes research requests
  └─ withGlobalLock('amend',  scanAmendments) ← [!claude] callouts
  └─ withGlobalLock('expand', scanExpands)    ← [!expand] callouts
  └─ withGlobalLock('revise', scanRevisions)  ← [!revise] callouts
  └─ withGlobalLock('lint',   scanLint)       ← lint-trigger.md

Each watcher:
  1. Scans vault directory for matching files
  2. Loads prompt template from vault (falls back to built-in)
  3. Substitutes tokens: {{VAULT_PATH}}, {{FILE_PATH}}, {{SEED_CONTENTS}}
  4. Calls runClaude(prompt, vaultPath, { budgetUsd, tools, model })
  5. claude-runner.js spawns: claude --print <prompt> --allowedTools ... --model ...
  6. On completion, notifier.js sends Discord/WhatsApp notification
```

## Callout detection — critical rules

Each callout watcher (amend, expand, revise) scans `research/**/*.md` for its trigger string. **Always use an anchored regex, never a bare string search:**

```js
// CORRECT — only matches a real Obsidian callout at line start
if (/^>\s*\[!claude\]/m.test(content)) results.push(file);

// WRONG — matches prose mentions like "unlike [!claude]..." in prompt files
if (content.includes('[!claude]')) results.push(file);
```

Why this matters: prompt template files (`*-prompt.md`) explain how callouts work and contain sentences like *"use a `[!claude]` callout to..."*. A bare `.includes()` matches those mentions on every poll cycle, triggering Claude invocations with no user intent and burning budget continuously.

## EXCLUDED_NAMES — the prompt file blocklist

Every callout watcher maintains an `EXCLUDED_NAMES` set of filenames that must never be processed, even if they contain callout syntax:

```js
const EXCLUDED_NAMES = new Set([
  'amend-prompt.md',
  'expand-prompt.md',
  'revise-prompt.md',
  'lint-prompt.md',
  'research-prompt.md',
]);
```

**Rule: when adding a new `*-prompt.md` template, add its filename to `EXCLUDED_NAMES` in EVERY existing watcher** — not just the one that owns it. New prompt files almost always mention other callout types in their explanatory text.

This rule was learned the hard way: a prompt file added in April 2026 was missing from two watchers' exclusion lists. Both watchers matched it on every 10-minute poll for several hours before the bug was caught.

## Adding a new watcher

Follow this checklist in order:

1. **Create the source file** at `services/bot/src/<name>-watcher.js`
   - Export a `scan<Name>()` function
   - Use anchored regex for callout detection
   - Add `EXCLUDED_NAMES` set (copy from an existing watcher; add the new prompt filename too)
   - Read the prompt template from vault with fallback to a built-in string
   - Call `runClaude(prompt, vaultPath, { budgetUsd, tools, model })`

2. **Wire into `index.js`**:
   ```js
   const { scanMyFeature } = require('./myfeature-watcher');
   // in pollAll():
   await withGlobalLock('myfeature', scanMyFeature);
   ```
   Add a poll interval env var if the watcher should be configurable.

3. **Add the prompt template** to `vault-scaffold/research/<name>-prompt.md`
   - Use `{{VAULT_PATH}}` and `{{FILE_PATH}}` tokens — never hardcode paths
   - Document what the watcher does and how to write good callout instructions
   - Add the new filename (`<name>-prompt.md`) to `EXCLUDED_NAMES` in every other watcher

4. **Add env vars** to `docker-compose.yml`:
   ```yaml
   - <NAME>_BUDGET_USD=1.00
   - <NAME>_MODEL=sonnet
   ```

5. **Add the prompt filename to every existing watcher's `EXCLUDED_NAMES`**:
   - `amend-watcher.js`
   - `expand-watcher.js`
   - `revise-watcher.js`
   - `lint-watcher.js`
   - (and any future watchers)

6. **Add an Obsidian template** (optional) to `templates/<name>-task.md` for quick insertion

7. **Write tests** in `tests/unit/<name>-watcher.test.js`:
   - Test callout detection regex (match + no-match cases)
   - Test EXCLUDED_NAMES correctly blocks prompt files
   - Mock `claude-runner.js` to avoid real Claude calls

8. **Update docs**:
   - Add a section to `docs/WORKFLOW.md`
   - Add a row to `docs/CONFIGURATION.md` for the new env vars
   - Update `README.md` feature list

## Race condition in inbox watcher

The inbox watcher moves the seed file to `research/processed/` **before** calling `runClaude()`. This is intentional:

Obsidian Sync conflict resolution can overwrite a file mid-run if the same file exists on both ends. If we set `status: processing` and left the file in `inbox/`, Obsidian could restore the original `status: pending` version during the Claude run, causing re-processing on the next tick.

Moving the file out of `inbox/` removes it from Obsidian's sync scope before Claude touches the vault. The file is only moved back to `inbox/` on error or `awaiting-input`.

## DRY_RUN mode

Set `DRY_RUN=true` to run the bot without calling Claude. Watchers scan the vault and log what they would process, but no Claude invocations happen and no vault files are modified.

Use this to:
- Verify callout detection is working
- Test vault scanning without spending API budget
- Smoke-test a new vault directory

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -e VAULT_PATH=/vault \
  -e DRY_RUN=true \
  -v /path/to/vault:/vault \
  ghcr.io/mrwong/rockybot:latest
```

## Running tests locally

Unit tests run against the root `package.json` (no Docker needed if you have Node.js):

```bash
npm install
npm test              # unit tests only (no Claude, no vault)
npm run test:integration  # real Claude calls, requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
```

Or via Docker:

```bash
docker run --rm -w /repo -v $(pwd):/repo node:20-alpine \
  sh -c "npm install && npm test"
```

Integration tests use `VAULT_PATH=/tmp/ci-test-vault` and are budget-capped to $0.10 per watcher. They require `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in the environment.

## Local development with docker-compose.dev.yml

The dev compose file builds from source and mounts vault to `/tmp/rockybot-dev-vault`, keeping it separate from any live vault:

```bash
cp .env.example .env
# set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in .env
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up
```

The source files in `services/bot/src/` are bind-mounted into the container, so JS changes take effect on the next poll without rebuilding the image.

To rebuild the image (needed for Dockerfile or scaffold changes):

```bash
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml build
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up
```

## Publishing a new version

Use the release script — it handles CHANGELOG, version bumps, tagging, and prompts before pushing:

```bash
./scripts/release.sh
```

The script shows commits since the last tag grouped by type, proposes a semver bump (major/minor/patch), asks you to confirm or override, then commits, tags, and optionally pushes. Pushing the tag triggers GitHub Actions to publish Docker images to GHCR.

See `CLAUDE.md` for the full versioning policy, including which commit prefixes map to which bump levels and what counts as a docs-only change (no bump).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Development Guide

## Architecture overview

rockybot is a Node.js process that polls an Obsidian vault directory every N seconds. On each poll it runs five watchers in sequence, serialized behind a global mutex (only one Claude invocation at a time):

```
index.js (poll loop)
  ├─ discordBot.init()                        ← connects Discord bot if DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH=true
  └─ withGlobalLock('inbox',  scanInbox)      ← processes research requests
  └─ withGlobalLock('amend',  scanAmendments) ← [!claude] callouts
  └─ withGlobalLock('expand', scanExpands)    ← [!expand] callouts
  └─ withGlobalLock('revise', scanRevisions)  ← [!revise] callouts
  └─ withGlobalLock('lint',   scanLint)       ← lint-trigger.md

Each watcher:
  1. Scans vault directory for matching files
  2. Loads prompt template from vault (falls back to built-in)
  3. Substitutes tokens: {{VAULT_PATH}}, {{FILE_PATH}}, {{SEED_CONTENTS}}
  4. Calls runClaude(prompt, vaultPath, { budgetUsd, tools, model, label })
  5. claude-runner.js spawns: claude --print <prompt> --allowedTools ... --model ...
     On subscription auth failure (interactive mode):
       login-runner.js spawns `claude login`, captures OAuth URL
       discord-bot.js posts URL + buttons, waits for user response or timeout
  6. On completion, notifier.js sends Discord/WhatsApp notification
```

## Callout detection — critical rules

Each callout watcher (amend, expand, revise) scans `research/**/*.md` for its trigger string. **Always use an anchored regex, never a bare string search:**

```js
// CORRECT — only matches a real Obsidian callout at line start
if (/^>\s*\[!claude\]/m.test(content)) results.push(file);

// WRONG — matches prose mentions like "unlike [!claude]..." in prompt files
if (content.includes('[!claude]')) results.push(file);
```

Why this matters: prompt template files (`*-prompt.md`) explain how callouts work and contain sentences like *"use a `[!claude]` callout to..."*. A bare `.includes()` matches those mentions on every poll cycle, triggering Claude invocations with no user intent and burning budget continuously.

## EXCLUDED_NAMES — the prompt file blocklist

Every callout watcher maintains an `EXCLUDED_NAMES` set of filenames that must never be processed, even if they contain callout syntax:

```js
const EXCLUDED_NAMES = new Set([
  'amend-prompt.md',
  'expand-prompt.md',
  'revise-prompt.md',
  'lint-prompt.md',
  'research-prompt.md',
]);
```

**Rule: when adding a new `*-prompt.md` template, add its filename to `EXCLUDED_NAMES` in EVERY existing watcher** — not just the one that owns it. New prompt files almost always mention other callout types in their explanatory text.

This rule was learned the hard way: a prompt file added in April 2026 was missing from two watchers' exclusion lists. Both watchers matched it on every 10-minute poll for several hours before the bug was caught.

## Adding a new watcher

Follow this checklist in order:

1. **Create the source file** at `services/bot/src/<name>-watcher.js`
   - Export a `scan<Name>()` function
   - Use anchored regex for callout detection
   - Add `EXCLUDED_NAMES` set (copy from an existing watcher; add the new prompt filename too)
   - Read the prompt template from vault with fallback to a built-in string
   - Call `runClaude(prompt, vaultPath, { budgetUsd, tools, model })`

2. **Wire into `index.js`**:
   ```js
   const { scanMyFeature } = require('./myfeature-watcher');
   // in pollAll():
   await withGlobalLock('myfeature', scanMyFeature);
   ```
   Add a poll interval env var if the watcher should be configurable.

3. **Add the prompt template** to `vault-scaffold/research/<name>-prompt.md`
   - Use `{{VAULT_PATH}}` and `{{FILE_PATH}}` tokens — never hardcode paths
   - Document what the watcher does and how to write good callout instructions
   - Add the new filename (`<name>-prompt.md`) to `EXCLUDED_NAMES` in every other watcher

4. **Add env vars** to `docker-compose.yml`:
   ```yaml
   - <NAME>_BUDGET_USD=1.00
   - <NAME>_MODEL=sonnet
   ```

5. **Add the prompt filename to every existing watcher's `EXCLUDED_NAMES`**:
   - `amend-watcher.js`
   - `expand-watcher.js`
   - `revise-watcher.js`
   - `lint-watcher.js`
   - (and any future watchers)

6. **Add an Obsidian template** (optional) to `templates/<name>-task.md` for quick insertion

7. **Write tests** in `tests/unit/<name>-watcher.test.js`:
   - Test callout detection regex (match + no-match cases)
   - Test EXCLUDED_NAMES correctly blocks prompt files
   - Mock `claude-runner.js` to avoid real Claude calls

8. **Update docs**:
   - Add a section to `docs/WORKFLOW.md`
   - Add a row to `docs/CONFIGURATION.md` for the new env vars
   - Update `README.md` feature list

## Race condition in inbox watcher

The inbox watcher moves the seed file to `research/processed/` **before** calling `runClaude()`. This is intentional:

Obsidian Sync conflict resolution can overwrite a file mid-run if the same file exists on both ends. If we set `status: processing` and left the file in `inbox/`, Obsidian could restore the original `status: pending` version during the Claude run, causing re-processing on the next tick.

Moving the file out of `inbox/` removes it from Obsidian's sync scope before Claude touches the vault. The file is only moved back to `inbox/` on error or `awaiting-input`.

## DRY_RUN mode

Set `DRY_RUN=true` to run the bot without calling Claude. Watchers scan the vault and log what they would process, but no Claude invocations happen and no vault files are modified.

Use this to:
- Verify callout detection is working
- Test vault scanning without spending API budget
- Smoke-test a new vault directory

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -e VAULT_PATH=/vault \
  -e DRY_RUN=true \
  -v /path/to/vault:/vault \
  ghcr.io/mrwong/rockybot:latest
```

## Running tests locally

Unit tests run against the root `package.json` (no Docker needed if you have Node.js):

```bash
npm install
npm test              # unit tests only (no Claude, no vault)
npm run test:integration  # real Claude calls, requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
```

Or via Docker:

```bash
docker run --rm -w /repo -v $(pwd):/repo node:20-alpine \
  sh -c "npm install && npm test"
```

Integration tests use `VAULT_PATH=/tmp/ci-test-vault` and are budget-capped to $0.10 per watcher. They require `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in the environment.

## Local development with docker-compose.dev.yml

The dev compose file builds from source and mounts vault to `/tmp/rockybot-dev-vault`, keeping it separate from any live vault:

```bash
cp .env.example .env
# set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in .env
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up
```

The source files in `services/bot/src/` are bind-mounted into the container, so JS changes take effect on the next poll without rebuilding the image.

To rebuild the image (needed for Dockerfile or scaffold changes):

```bash
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml build
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up
```

## Publishing a new version

Use the release script — it handles CHANGELOG, version bumps, tagging, and prompts before pushing:

```bash
./scripts/release.sh
```

The script shows commits since the last tag grouped by type, proposes a semver bump (major/minor/patch), asks you to confirm or override, then commits, tags, and optionally pushes. Pushing the tag triggers GitHub Actions to publish Docker images to GHCR.

See `CLAUDE.md` for the full versioning policy, including which commit prefixes map to which bump levels and what counts as a docs-only change (no bump).

      - ./services/obsidian-bridge/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
    labels:
      - "traefik.enable=true"
      - "traefik.http.routers.notes-web.entrypoints=websecure"
      - "traefik.http.routers.notes-web.rule=Host(`notes.${DOMAIN_NAME}`)"
      - "traefik.http.routers.notes-web.tls=true"

# Centralized secrets file for boughtmyown.cloud homelab
# Copy this file to homelab.env and fill in your actual secrets

# Domain configuration
DOMAIN_NAME=boughtmyown.cloud

#-----------------------------------------------
# Proxmox credentials

PROXMOX_API_URL=https://proxmox.local:8006/api2/json
REDACTED
REDACTED

#-----------------------------------------------

VM_USERNAME=ubuntu
VM_PASSWORD=your-secure-password

#-----------------------------------------------
# Cloudflare configuration
#-----------------------------------------------
REDACTED
CLOUDFLARE_EMAIL=your-email@example.com
REDACTED

# Comma-separated list of subdomains to manage with DDNS
CLOUDFLARE_SUBDOMAINS=home,traefik,nextcloud

#-----------------------------------------------
# Traefik configuration
#-----------------------------------------------
# Generate with: docker run --rm httpd:2.4-alpine htpasswd -nbB admin "your-secure-password" | cut -d ":" -f 2
TRAEFIK_BASIC_AUTH=admin:$$2y$$05$$example-hashed-password

# Dozzle authentication
# Dozzle uses file-based authentication with users.yml
# Generate users.yml by running: ./generate-dozzle-users.sh
# The users.yml file will be stored in /mnt/nfs/MainStoragePool/docker_data/dozzle/

#-----------------------------------------------
# TrueNAS configuration
#-----------------------------------------------
# TrueNAS host and API key
TRUENAS_HOST=truenas.local
TRUENAS_USER='user'
TRUENAS_PASSWORD='password'

#-----------------------------------------------
# Samba configuration
#-----------------------------------------------
# Password for Samba user (will be set for the ubuntu user)
SAMBA_PASSWORD='your-secure-samba-password'

#-----------------------------------------------
# Tailscale configuration
#-----------------------------------------------
# Generate an auth key at https://login.tailscale.com/admin/settings/keys
TAILSCALE_AUTH_KEY=tskey-auth-your-key-here
TAILSCALE_HOSTNAME=dockercompute

#-----------------------------------------------
# GitHub deployment key
#-----------------------------------------------
# Public key to add to GitHub repository deploy keys
GITHUB_DEPLOY_KEY_PUB="ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIO14sfdadf6Q05tV tim@Desktop"
# Path to the private key file
GITHUB_DEPLOY_KEY_PATH="/home/tim/.ssh/boughtmyowncloud"

#-----------------------------------------------
# Mail-Archiver Configuration
#-----------------------------------------------
# Database credentials for Mail-Archiver PostgreSQL
REDACTED  # Strong password for the database

# Web interface admin credentials
MAIL_ARCHIVER_ADMIN_USER=admin                      # Admin username for web interface
REDACTED  # Strong password for admin access

# Optional: OIDC/OAuth2 Configuration (uncomment and configure if using OIDC)
# MAIL_ARCHIVER_OAUTH_ENABLED=false
# MAIL_ARCHIVER_OAUTH_AUTHORITY=https://your-oidc-provider.com
# REDACTED
# REDACTED

# Optional: Email account credentials (add these when setting up email accounts in the web UI)
# MAIL_ARCHIVER_IMAP_SERVER=imap.example.com
# MAIL_ARCHIVER_IMAP_PORT=993
# MAIL_ARCHIVER_IMAP_USER=your-email@example.com
# REDACTED
# MAIL_ARCHIVER_IMAP_USE_SSL=true

#-----------------------------------------------
# Other Service Credentials
#-----------------------------------------------
# Add additional service credentials as needed

#-----------------------------------------------
# Nextcloud configuration
#-----------------------------------------------
# PostgreSQL database password for Nextcloud
REDACTED
# Nextcloud admin user credentials
NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER=admin
REDACTED

#-----------------------------------------------
# Stash configuration
#-----------------------------------------------
# API key for Stash (generate in Stash web UI under Settings > Security > Authentication)
STASH_API_KEY=your-stash-api-key-here

#-----------------------------------------------
# Obsidian Headless (vault sync daemon)
#-----------------------------------------------
# Auth is handled via persisted config volume — no credentials needed here.
# Run the one-time setup script after first deploy:
#   ./machines/dockercompute/docker/services/obsidian-headless/setup.sh

#-----------------------------------------------
# Authelia SSO
#-----------------------------------------------
# Generate each with: python3 -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_hex(32))"
AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET=your-session-secret-here
REDACTED
AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET=your-jwt-secret-here

# Notes web viewer auth is now handled by Authelia (see above)
# NOTES_BASIC_AUTH is no longer required

#-----------------------------------------------
# Anthropic / Claude (research-bot)
#-----------------------------------------------
# API key — used as fallback when subscription billing is unavailable.
# Get from: console.anthropic.com → API Keys
REDACTED

# Subscription billing mode — uses your Claude Pro/Max OAuth session as the
# primary billing path (flat-rate), falling back to ANTHROPIC_API_KEY when
# the 5-hour rolling window is exhausted or auth has lapsed.
# One-time setup: run `claude login` on dockercompute as the ubuntu user.
# If unset, defaults to true. Set to false to use API key billing only.
CLAUDE_SUBSCRIPTION_MODE=true

#-----------------------------------------------
# Research-bot notifications (optional)
#-----------------------------------------------
# Set one or both to receive Discord/WhatsApp messages when a watcher completes or errors.
# Leave blank (or omit) to disable notifications entirely.

# Discord: create a webhook in Server Settings → Integrations → Webhooks
DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL=

# WhatsApp via Twilio: requires a Twilio account with WhatsApp sandbox or approved sender
# TWILIO_FROM format: whatsapp:+14155238886  (Twilio sandbox number or approved sender)
# TWILIO_TO format:   whatsapp:+1XXXXXXXXXX  (your number, opted in to sandbox)
TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID=
TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN=
TWILIO_FROM=
TWILIO_TO=

# Authelia SSO
AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET=$AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET
AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY=$AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY
AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET=$AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET

# Anthropic API (research-bot container)
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

# Research-bot notifications (optional — leave blank to disable)
DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL=$DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL
TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID=$TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID
TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN=$TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN
TWILIO_FROM=$TWILIO_FROM
TWILIO_TO=$TWILIO_TO

EOF
# Append bcrypt hash values with printf to prevent $ expansion in heredoc
printf 'TRAEFIK_BASIC_AUTH=%s\n' "$TRAEFIK_BASIC_AUTH" >> "$ENV_FILE"
printf 'NOTES_BASIC_AUTH=%s\n' "$NOTES_BASIC_AUTH" >> "$ENV_FILE"
chmod 600 "$ENV_FILE"

# Sync Traefik dynamic config to NFS (source of truth is the repo)
echo "Syncing Traefik dynamic config to NFS..."
TRAEFIK_CONFIG_SRC="$DOCKER_DIR/traefik/config"

toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/bin/bash

# Direct deployment script for Docker Compose services
# This script is designed to be run directly on the Docker Compute VM
# It handles Docker Compose deployment without using Ansible

set -e

# Get script directory and set paths
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
DOCKER_DIR="$SCRIPT_DIR"
ENV_TEMPLATE="$DOCKER_DIR/templates/docker.env.j2"
ENV_FILE="$DOCKER_DIR/.env"

# Parse command line arguments
SPECIFIC_SERVICE=""
FORCE_RESTART=false

while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
  case $1 in
    -s|--service)
      SPECIFIC_SERVICE="$2"
      shift 2
      ;;
    -f|--force)
      FORCE_RESTART=true
      shift
      ;;
    *)
      echo "Unknown option: $1"
      echo "Usage: $0 [-s|--service SERVICE_NAME] [-f|--force]"
      echo "  -s, --service: Deploy only a specific service"
      echo "  -f, --force: Force restart of all services"
      exit 1
      ;;
  esac
done

# Check if Docker is running
if ! systemctl is-active --quiet docker; then
  echo "Error: Docker is not running. Please start Docker first."
  exit 1
fi

# Make all shell scripts executable
echo "Making scripts executable..."
find "$DOCKER_DIR" -name "*.sh" -exec chmod +x {} \;

# Source the secrets file from the project root
PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/../../../" && pwd)"
SECRETS_FILE="$PROJECT_ROOT/secrets/homelab.env"

# If we're running on the Docker Compute VM via GitHub Actions, we might have secrets in environment variables
# In that case, we'll use those instead of the secrets file
if [[ "$(hostname)" == "dockercompute" ]] && [[ -n "$GITHUB_ACTIONS" ]]; then
  echo "Running on Docker Compute VM via GitHub Actions - using environment variables"
  # The environment variables should already be set by GitHub Actions secrets
  # We'll just verify that required ones are present
  if [[ -z "$DOMAIN_NAME" ]]; then
    echo "Error: DOMAIN_NAME environment variable is not set"
    exit 1
  fi
else
  # We're running locally, so we need to source the secrets file
  if [ -f "$SECRETS_FILE" ]; then
    echo "Loading secrets from $SECRETS_FILE..."
    set -a  # automatically export all variables
    source "$SECRETS_FILE"
    set +a  # disable auto-export
  else
    echo "Error: Secrets file not found at $SECRETS_FILE"
    echo "Please ensure the secrets file exists before running this script."
    exit 1
  fi
fi

# Create or update .env file for Docker Compose
echo "Creating .env file for Docker Compose..."
cat > "$ENV_FILE" << EOF
# Generated by deploy-direct.sh on $(date)
# DO NOT EDIT MANUALLY - This file is regenerated on each deployment

# Domain configuration
DOMAIN_NAME=$DOMAIN_NAME

# Cloudflare configuration
CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY=$CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY
CLOUDFLARE_EMAIL=$CLOUDFLARE_EMAIL
CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID=$CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID

# Traefik configuration (written below heredoc to avoid $ expansion)

# Plex configuration
PLEX_CLAIM_TOKEN=$PLEX_CLAIM_TOKEN

# Nextcloud configuration
NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD=$NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD
NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER=$NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER
NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD=$NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD

# Authelia SSO
AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET=$AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET
AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY=$AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY
AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET=$AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET

# Anthropic API (research-bot container)
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

# Research-bot notifications (optional — leave blank to disable)
DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL=$DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL
TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID=$TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID
TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN=$TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN
TWILIO_FROM=$TWILIO_FROM
TWILIO_TO=$TWILIO_TO

EOF
# Append bcrypt hash values with printf to prevent $ expansion in heredoc
printf 'TRAEFIK_BASIC_AUTH=%s\n' "$TRAEFIK_BASIC_AUTH" >> "$ENV_FILE"
printf 'NOTES_BASIC_AUTH=%s\n' "$NOTES_BASIC_AUTH" >> "$ENV_FILE"
chmod 600 "$ENV_FILE"

# Sync Traefik dynamic config to NFS (source of truth is the repo)
echo "Syncing Traefik dynamic config to NFS..."
TRAEFIK_CONFIG_SRC="$DOCKER_DIR/traefik/config"
TRAEFIK_CONFIG_DST="/mnt/nfs/MainStoragePool/docker_data/traefik/config"
mkdir -p "$TRAEFIK_CONFIG_DST"
cp -f "$TRAEFIK_CONFIG_SRC"/*.yml "$TRAEFIK_CONFIG_DST/"

# Deploy specific service if requested
if [ -n "$SPECIFIC_SERVICE" ]; then
  echo "Deploying specific service: $SPECIFIC_SERVICE"
  
  # Check if service exists in docker-compose.yml
  if ! grep -q "$SPECIFIC_SERVICE:" "$DOCKER_DIR/docker-compose.yml"; then
    echo "Error: Service '$SPECIFIC_SERVICE' not found in docker-compose.yml"
    exit 1
  fi
  
  # Stop and remove existing container if it exists
  echo "Stopping existing container if it exists..."
  docker stop "$SPECIFIC_SERVICE" 2>/dev/null || true
  docker rm "$SPECIFIC_SERVICE" 2>/dev/null || true

  # Rebuild or pull depending on whether service uses a local build context
  if grep -A5 "$SPECIFIC_SERVICE:" "$DOCKER_DIR/docker-compose.yml" | grep -q "build:"; then
    echo "Build context detected — rebuilding image for $SPECIFIC_SERVICE"
    cd "$DOCKER_DIR" && docker compose build "$SPECIFIC_SERVICE"
  else
    echo "Image-based service — pulling latest from registry"
    cd "$DOCKER_DIR" && docker compose pull "$SPECIFIC_SERVICE"
  fi

  # Start the service
  echo "Starting service: $SPECIFIC_SERVICE"
  cd "$DOCKER_DIR" && docker compose up -d "$SPECIFIC_SERVICE"
else
  # Full deployment
  echo "Performing full deployment of all services..."

  if [ "$FORCE_RESTART" = true ]; then
    echo "Force restart enabled: All services will be restarted"
    cd "$DOCKER_DIR" && docker compose build && docker compose down && docker compose up -d --remove-orphans
  else
    # Pull latest images and recreate containers
    echo "Pulling latest images..."
    cd "$DOCKER_DIR" && docker compose build && docker compose pull

    echo "Starting services..."
    cd "$DOCKER_DIR" && docker compose up -d --remove-orphans
  fi
fi

# notes-web mounts nginx.conf as a file bind mount; git pull creates a new inode
# so docker compose up -d won't notice the change. Always recreate it.
echo "Reloading notes-web nginx config..."
cd "$DOCKER_DIR" && docker compose up -d --force-recreate notes-web

# Prune dangling images left over from this deployment
echo "Pruning dangling images..."
docker image prune -f

# Show status of Docker services
echo "Current Docker services status:"
cd "$DOCKER_DIR" && docker compose ps

echo "Deployment completed successfully!"

toolUseResult.originalFile:
name: GitOps Sync

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      deploy_target:
        description: 'Deployment target (dockercompute, all)'
        required: true
        default: 'dockercompute'
      restart_services:
        description: 'Restart all services after deployment'
        type: boolean
        default: false

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install yamllint
        run: pip install yamllint

      - name: Validate docker-compose YAML
        run: yamllint -c .yamllint.yml machines/dockercompute/docker/docker-compose.yml

      - name: Validate docker-compose syntax
        # Uses dummy values so variable substitution succeeds without real secrets
        run: |
          docker compose \
            -f machines/dockercompute/docker/docker-compose.yml \
            config --quiet
        env:
          DOMAIN_NAME: example.com
          CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY: dummy
          CLOUDFLARE_EMAIL: dummy@example.com
          CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID: dummy
          TRAEFIK_BASIC_AUTH: "admin:$$2y$$05$$dummyhash"
          PLEX_CLAIM_TOKEN: dummy
          NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD: dummy
          NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER: admin
          NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD: dummy
          STASH_API_KEY: dummy
          NOTES_BASIC_AUTH: "admin:$$2y$$05$$dummyhash"
          AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET: dummy
          AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY: dummy
          AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET: dummy
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: dummy

  deploy:
    needs: validate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up SSH
        uses: webfactory/ssh-agent@v0.7.0
        with:
          ssh-private-key: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_SSH_KEY }}

      - name: Setup Tailscale
        uses: tailscale/github-action@v2
        with:
          oauth-client-id: ${{ secrets.TS_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID }}
          oauth-secret: ${{ secrets.TS_OAUTH_SECRET }}
          tags: tag:github-actions

      - name: Deploy to Docker Compute VM
        env:
          DOCKER_VM_TAILSCALE_IP: ${{ secrets.DOCKER_VM_TAILSCALE_IP }}
          DOCKER_VM_USER: ${{ secrets.DOCKER_VM_USER }}
          DOMAIN_NAME: ${{ secrets.DOMAIN_NAME }}
          CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY }}
          CLOUDFLARE_EMAIL: ${{ secrets.CLOUDFLARE_EMAIL }}
          CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID: ${{ secrets.CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID }}
          TRAEFIK_BASIC_AUTH: ${{ secrets.TRAEFIK_BASIC_AUTH }}
          TAILSCALE_AUTH_KEY: ${{ secrets.TAILSCALE_AUTH_KEY }}
          TAILSCALE_HOSTNAME: ${{ secrets.TAILSCALE_HOSTNAME }}
          PLEX_CLAIM_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PLEX_CLAIM_TOKEN }}
          NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD }}
          NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER: ${{ secrets.NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER }}
          NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD }}
          STASH_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.STASH_API_KEY }}
          NOTES_BASIC_AUTH: ${{ secrets.NOTES_BASIC_AUTH }}
          AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET: ${{ secrets.AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET }}
          AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY: ${{ secrets.AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY }}
          AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET: ${{ secrets.AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET }}
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL }}
          TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID: ${{ secrets.TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID }}
          TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN }}
          TWILIO_FROM: ${{ secrets.TWILIO_FROM }}
          TWILIO_TO: ${{ secrets.TWILIO_TO }}
        run: |
          echo "Pulling latest changes on Docker Compute VM..."
          ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new $DOCKER_VM_USER@$DOCKER_VM_TAILSCALE_IP \
            "cd /home/$DOCKER_VM_USER/boughtmyowncloud && git pull"

          echo "Running direct deployment script..."
          DEPLOY_FLAGS=""
          if [ "${{ github.event.inputs.restart_services }}" == "true" ]; then
            DEPLOY_FLAGS="-f"
          fi

          ssh $DOCKER_VM_USER@$DOCKER_VM_TAILSCALE_IP "
            export GITHUB_ACTIONS=true
            export DOMAIN_NAME=\"$DOMAIN_NAME\"
            export CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY=\"$CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY\"
            export CLOUDFLARE_EMAIL=\"$CLOUDFLARE_EMAIL\"
            export CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID=\"$CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID\"
            export TRAEFIK_BASIC_AUTH=\"$TRAEFIK_BASIC_AUTH\"
            export TAILSCALE_AUTH_KEY=\"$TAILSCALE_AUTH_KEY\"
            export TAILSCALE_HOSTNAME=\"$TAILSCALE_HOSTNAME\"
            export PLEX_CLAIM_TOKEN=\"$PLEX_CLAIM_TOKEN\"
            export NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD=\"$NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD\"
            export NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER=\"$NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER\"
            export NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD=\"$NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD\"
            export STASH_API_KEY=\"$STASH_API_KEY\"
            export NOTES_BASIC_AUTH=\"$NOTES_BASIC_AUTH\"
            export AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET=\"$AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET\"
            export AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY=\"$AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY\"
            export AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET=\"$AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET\"
            export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=\"$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY\"
            export DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL=\"$DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL\"
            export TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID=\"$TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID\"
            export TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN=\"$TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN\"
            export TWILIO_FROM=\"$TWILIO_FROM\"
            export TWILIO_TO=\"$TWILIO_TO\"
            cd /home/$DOCKER_VM_USER/boughtmyowncloud/machines/dockercompute/docker
            ./deploy-direct.sh $DEPLOY_FLAGS
          "

      - name: Verify deployment
        env:
          DOCKER_VM_TAILSCALE_IP: ${{ secrets.DOCKER_VM_TAILSCALE_IP }}
          DOCKER_VM_USER: ${{ secrets.DOCKER_VM_USER }}
        run: |
          echo "Verifying Docker services are running..."
          ssh $DOCKER_VM_USER@$DOCKER_VM_TAILSCALE_IP \
            "docker ps --format '{{.Names}} - {{.Status}}' | grep -v 'Exited'"
        continue-on-error: true

      - name: Notify Discord — success
        if: success()
        env:
          DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL }}
        run: |
          [[ -z "$DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL" ]] && exit 0
          SHORT_SHA="${{ github.sha }}"
          SHORT_SHA="${SHORT_SHA:0:7}"
          curl -s -o /dev/null -X POST "$DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d "{\"embeds\":[{\"title\":\"✅ Deploy succeeded\",\"description\":\"[\`${SHORT_SHA}\`](${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/commit/${{ github.sha }}) by ${{ github.actor }}\",\"color\":3066993,\"url\":\"${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/actions/runs/${{ github.run_id }}\"}]}"

      - name: Notify Discord — failure
        if: failure()
        env:
          DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL }}
        run: |
          [[ -z "$DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL" ]] && exit 0
          SHORT_SHA="${{ github.sha }}"
          SHORT_SHA="${SHORT_SHA:0:7}"
          curl -s -o /dev/null -X POST "$DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d "{\"embeds\":[{\"title\":\"❌ Deploy failed\",\"description\":\"[\`${SHORT_SHA}\`](${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/commit/${{ github.sha }}) by ${{ github.actor }}\",\"color\":15158332,\"url\":\"${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/actions/runs/${{ github.run_id }}\"}]}"

          TAILSCALE_AUTH_KEY: ${{ secrets.TAILSCALE_AUTH_KEY }}
          TAILSCALE_HOSTNAME: ${{ secrets.TAILSCALE_HOSTNAME }}
          PLEX_CLAIM_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PLEX_CLAIM_TOKEN }}
          NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD }}
          NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER: ${{ secrets.NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER }}
          NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD }}
          STASH_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.STASH_API_KEY }}
          NOTES_BASIC_AUTH: ${{ secrets.NOTES_BASIC_AUTH }}
          AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET: ${{ secrets.AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET }}
          AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY: ${{ secrets.AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY }}
          AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET: ${{ secrets.AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET }}
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL }}
          TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID: ${{ secrets.TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID }}
          TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN }}
          TWILIO_FROM: ${{ secrets.TWILIO_FROM }}
          TWILIO_TO: ${{ secrets.TWILIO_TO }}
          DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH: ${{ secrets.DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH }}
          DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN }}
          DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID: ${{ secrets.DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID }}
          DISCORD_AUTH_TIMEOUT_MINUTES: ${{ secrets.DISCORD_AUTH_TIMEOUT_MINUTES }}
        run: |
          echo "Pulling latest changes on Docker Compute VM..."
          ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new $DOCKER_VM_USER@$DOCKER_VM_TAILSCALE_IP \
            "cd /home/$DOCKER_VM_USER/boughtmyowncloud && git pull"

          echo "Running direct deployment script..."
          DEPLOY_FLAGS=""
          if [ "${{ github.event.inputs.restart_services }}" == "true" ]; then
            DEPLOY_FLAGS="-f"
          fi

          ssh $DOCKER_VM_USER@$DOCKER_VM_TAILSCALE_IP "
            export GITHUB_ACTIONS=true
            export DOMAIN_NAME=\"$DOMAIN_NAME\"
            export CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY=\"$CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY\"
            export CLOUDFLARE_EMAIL=\"$CLOUDFLARE_EMAIL\"
            export CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID=\"$CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID\"
            export TRAEFIK_BASIC_AUTH=\"$TRAEFIK_BASIC_AUTH\"
            export TAILSCALE_AUTH_KEY=\"$TAILSCALE_AUTH_KEY\"
            export TAILSCALE_HOSTNAME=\"$TAILSCALE_HOSTNAME\"
            export PLEX_CLAIM_TOKEN=\"$PLEX_CLAIM_TOKEN\"
            export NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD=\"$NEXTCLOUD_DB_PASSWORD\"
            export NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER=\"$NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER\"
            export NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD=\"$NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD\"
            export STASH_API_KEY=\"$STASH_API_KEY\"
            export NOTES_BASIC_AUTH=\"$NOTES_BASIC_AUTH\"
            export AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET=\"$AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET\"
            export AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY=\"$AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY\"
            export AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET=\"$AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET\"
            export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=\"$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY\"
            export DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL=\"$DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL\"
            export TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID=\"$TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID\"
            export TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN=\"$TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN\"
            export TWILIO_FROM=\"$TWILIO_FROM\"
            export TWILIO_TO=\"$TWILIO_TO\"
            cd /home/$DOCKER_VM_USER/boughtmyowncloud/machines/dockercompute/docker
            ./deploy-direct.sh $DEPLOY_FLAGS
          "

      - name: Verify deployment
        env:
          DOCKER_VM_TAILSCALE_IP: ${{ secrets.DOCKER_VM_TAILSCALE_IP }}
          DOCKER_VM_USER: ${{ secrets.DOCKER_VM_USER }}
        run: |
          echo "Verifying Docker services are running..."
          ssh $DOCKER_VM_USER@$DOCKER_VM_TAILSCALE_IP \
            "docker ps --format '{{.Names}} - {{.Status}}' | grep -v 'Exited'"
        continue-on-error: true

      - name: Notify Discord — success
        if: success()
        env:
          DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL }}
        run: |
          [[ -z "$DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL" ]] && exit 0
          SHORT_SHA="${{ github.sha }}"
          SHORT_SHA="${SHORT_SHA:0:7}"
          curl -s -o /dev/null -X POST "$DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d "{\"embeds\":[{\"title\":\"✅ Deploy succeeded\",\"description\":\"[\`${SHORT_SHA}\`](${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/commit/${{ github.sha }}) by ${{ github.actor }}\",\"color\":3066993,\"url\":\"${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/actions/runs/${{ github.run_id }}\"}]}"

      - name: Notify Discord — failure
        if: failure()
        env:
          DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL }}
        run: |
          [[ -z "$DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL" ]] && exit 0
          SHORT_SHA="${{ github.sha }}"
          SHORT_SHA="${SHORT_SHA:0:7}"
          curl -s -o /dev/null -X POST "$DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d "{\"embeds\":[{\"title\":\"❌ Deploy failed\",\"description\":\"[\`${SHORT_SHA}\`](${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/commit/${{ github.sha }}) by ${{ github.actor }}\",\"color\":15158332,\"url\":\"${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/actions/runs/${{ github.run_id }}\"}]}"

content.text:
Done. Here are the setup instructions:

---

## Discord bot setup

### 1. Create the application

1. Go to **discord.com/developers/applications** → **New Application**
2. Name it something like `rockybot` → **Create**
3. Under **Bot** (left sidebar) → **Reset Token** → copy the token — this is `DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN`

### 2. Configure permissions

Still under **Bot**:
- Scroll down to **Bot Permissions** and enable: **Send Messages**, **Read Message History**
- No privileged intents are needed (buttons don't require Message Content intent)

### 3. Add the bot to your server

1. Under **OAuth2 → URL Generator** (left sidebar)
2. Scopes: tick **bot**
3. Bot Permissions: tick **Send Messages** + **Read Message History**
4. Copy the generated URL, open it in a browser, select your Discord server → **Authorise**

### 4. Get the channel ID

1. In Discord: **User Settings → Advanced → Developer Mode** → toggle on
2. Right-click the channel you want bot messages in → **Copy Channel ID** — this is `DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID`

### 5. Add secrets to the homelab

In your `secrets/homelab.env` (not the `.example`):

```env
DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH=true
DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN=your-bot-token-here
DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID=REDACTED
DISCORD_AUTH_TIMEOUT_MINUTES=5
```

Then add `DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN` and `DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID` as GitHub Actions secrets in the boughtmyowncloud repo (Settings → Secrets → Actions) so the GitOps pipeline can inject them.

### 6. Deploy

Once the secrets are in place, trigger a GitOps deploy (push to main or manually run the workflow). The `research-bot` container will restart with the new env vars and connect to Discord on startup. You'll know it's working when you see the bot appear as **Online** in your server's member list.

To test it: temporarily break the Claude session (e.g. delete `~/.claude.json` on the host), wait for the next research poll, and the interactive auth message should appear in your Discord channel.

Ready to commit the changes?

- Adds DISCORD_INTERACTIVE_AUTH, DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN, DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID,
  and DISCORD_AUTH_TIMEOUT_MINUTES throughout the secrets/deploy chain
- Interactive auth defaults to true — on subscription failure the bot
  posts an OAuth link to Discord rather than silently falling back

To activate: add DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN and DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID to homelab.env
and as GitHub Actions secrets, then trigger a GitOps deploy.
See rockybot docs/NOTIFICATIONS.md for Discord bot setup steps.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.content:
1	# Obsidian Notes Stack
2	
3	See also: [ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md), [AUTHELIA.md](AUTHELIA.md), [RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md](RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md)
4	
5	This document covers how the Obsidian vault is synced, watched, and published to the web.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Architecture
10	
11	```
12	Obsidian app (mobile/desktop)
13	  │  Obsidian Sync (paid, official)
14	  ▼
15	obsidian-headless  (Node.js daemon — ob sync --continuous)
16	  │  bidirectional sync
17	  ▼
18	Local vault: /home/ubuntu/docker-volumes/obsidian-vault/
19	  │
20	  ├── obsidian-bridge  (Node.js)
21	  │     └── chokidar watcher → debounced Quartz build (3s)
22	  │
23	  ├── research-bot  (Node.js)
24	  │     └── polls inbox/, amend callouts, lint trigger
25	  │
26	  ▼
27	notes-web  (nginx)
28	  notes.boughtmyown.cloud — authenticated via Authelia SSO
29	  Serves Quartz static site from NFS quartz-output (read-only)
30	```
31	
32	`obsidian-headless` is the sync bridge between Obsidian Sync (cloud) and the local vault on disk. All other services (bridge, research-bot) read and write the local vault directly — no NFS or Samba involved.
33	
34	---
35	
36	## Services
37	
38	| Container | Image | Subdomain | Auth |
39	|---|---|---|---|
40	| `obsidian-headless` | local build | — (internal) | Session token in `/config` volume |
41	| `obsidian-bridge` | local build | — (internal) | — |
42	| `notes-web` | `nginx:alpine` | `notes.boughtmyown.cloud` | Authelia SSO |
43	
44	---
45	
46	## Paths
47	
48	| Purpose | Path |
49	|---|---|
50	| Vault (local) | `/home/ubuntu/docker-volumes/obsidian-vault/` |
51	| Headless config / session | `/home/ubuntu/docker-volumes/obsidian-config/` |
52	| Quartz output (NFS, served by nginx) | `/mnt/nfs/MainStoragePool/docker_data/obsidian/quartz-output/` |
53	
54	---
55	
56	## obsidian-headless service
57	
58	Runs `ob sync --continuous` against the local vault, keeping it in bidirectional sync with Obsidian Sync. No credentials in environment variables — auth state is persisted in the `/config` volume after one-time interactive setup.
59	
60	### One-time setup (after first deploy)
61	
62	```bash
63	# On the dockercompute VM:
64	mkdir -p /home/ubuntu/docker-volumes/obsidian-vault
65	mkdir -p /home/ubuntu/docker-volumes/obsidian-config
66	
67	# Migrate existing vault content from NFS (first time only):
68	rsync -av /mnt/nfs/MainStoragePool/other/obsidian-main/ \
69	         /home/ubuntu/docker-volumes/obsidian-vault/
70	
71	# Interactive login + vault link:
72	cd /home/ubuntu/src/boughtmyowncloud
73	./machines/dockercompute/docker/services/obsidian-headless/setup.sh
74	```
75	
76	The setup script walks through `ob login`, lists your remote vaults, and calls `ob sync-setup` to link the local path. After that the container syncs automatically on restart with no further input.
77	
78	---
79	
80	## obsidian-bridge service
81	
82	Watches the local vault and rebuilds the Quartz static site on change.
83	
84	1. On startup: copies vault scaffold files to the local vault (new files only), runs initial Quartz build.
85	2. Watches the vault with chokidar (polling every 5s).
86	3. On any `.md` change: schedules a debounced Quartz rebuild (3s).
87	
88	### Quartz build
89	
90	Quartz v4.4.0 is baked into the Docker image at build time. On each build:
91	1. `rsync` copies the vault to `quartz-src/content/` (excluding `.obsidian/`)
92	2. `npx quartz build --output /quartz-output` runs
93	3. `chmod -R o+r` is applied to the NFS quartz-output so nginx can serve the files
94	
95	### Vault scaffold
96	
97	Seed files (templates, research prompt, index pages) live at `machines/dockercompute/docker/services/obsidian-bridge/vault-scaffold/`. The bridge copies them to the local vault on startup (new files only — existing files are never overwritten).
98	
99	---
100	
101	## Research Workflow
102	
103	The stack includes an automated research pipeline. See [RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md](RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md) for full details.
104	
105	**Quick summary:** In Obsidian, use the Templater **research-request** template to create a new note in `research/inbox/`. Fill it in, then change `status: draft` → `status: pending`. The research-bot picks it up on the next poll (~10 min) and writes structured output into `research/<topic>/`. Changes flow back to your Obsidian clients via obsidian-headless → Obsidian Sync.
106	
107	---
108	
109	## Troubleshooting
110	
111	| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
112	|---|---|---|
113	| `notes.boughtmyown.cloud` 403 | NFS permission bits stripped on quartz-output | `chmod -R o+r /mnt/nfs/MainStoragePool/docker_data/obsidian/quartz-output` |
114	| Notes site not updating after vault change | Bridge not running or build failed | `docker logs obsidian-bridge --tail 30` |
115	| Scaffold files missing in Obsidian | Seeding failed on startup | `docker restart obsidian-bridge` |
116	| Vault changes not reaching Obsidian clients | obsidian-headless not syncing | `docker logs obsidian-headless --tail 30` |
117	| obsidian-headless waiting for setup | One-time setup not completed | Run `./machines/dockercompute/docker/services/obsidian-headless/setup.sh` |
118	| obsidian-headless auth expired | Session token stale | `docker exec -it obsidian-headless ob login`, then restart |
119	| New vault `.md` not triggering rebuild | Chokidar polling lag | Bridge polls every 5s with 3s debounce; wait ~10s |
120

file.content:
# Obsidian Notes Stack

See also: [ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md), [AUTHELIA.md](AUTHELIA.md), [RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md](RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md)

This document covers how the Obsidian vault is synced, watched, and published to the web.

---

## Architecture

```
Obsidian app (mobile/desktop)
  │  Obsidian Sync (paid, official)
  ▼
obsidian-headless  (Node.js daemon — ob sync --continuous)
  │  bidirectional sync
  ▼
Local vault: /home/ubuntu/docker-volumes/obsidian-vault/
  │
  ├── obsidian-bridge  (Node.js)
  │     └── chokidar watcher → debounced Quartz build (3s)
  │
  ├── research-bot  (Node.js)
  │     └── polls inbox/, amend callouts, lint trigger
  │
  ▼
notes-web  (nginx)
  notes.boughtmyown.cloud — authenticated via Authelia SSO
  Serves Quartz static site from NFS quartz-output (read-only)
```

`obsidian-headless` is the sync bridge between Obsidian Sync (cloud) and the local vault on disk. All other services (bridge, research-bot) read and write the local vault directly — no NFS or Samba involved.

---

## Services

| Container | Image | Subdomain | Auth |
|---|---|---|---|
| `obsidian-headless` | local build | — (internal) | Session token in `/config` volume |
| `obsidian-bridge` | local build | — (internal) | — |
| `notes-web` | `nginx:alpine` | `notes.boughtmyown.cloud` | Authelia SSO |

---

## Paths

| Purpose | Path |
|---|---|
| Vault (local) | `/home/ubuntu/docker-volumes/obsidian-vault/` |
| Headless config / session | `/home/ubuntu/docker-volumes/obsidian-config/` |
| Quartz output (NFS, served by nginx) | `/mnt/nfs/MainStoragePool/docker_data/obsidian/quartz-output/` |

---

## obsidian-headless service

Runs `ob sync --continuous` against the local vault, keeping it in bidirectional sync with Obsidian Sync. No credentials in environment variables — auth state is persisted in the `/config` volume after one-time interactive setup.

### One-time setup (after first deploy)

```bash
# On the dockercompute VM:
mkdir -p /home/ubuntu/docker-volumes/obsidian-vault
mkdir -p /home/ubuntu/docker-volumes/obsidian-config

# Migrate existing vault content from NFS (first time only):
rsync -av /mnt/nfs/MainStoragePool/other/obsidian-main/ \
         /home/ubuntu/docker-volumes/obsidian-vault/

# Interactive login + vault link:
cd /home/ubuntu/src/boughtmyowncloud
./machines/dockercompute/docker/services/obsidian-headless/setup.sh
```

The setup script walks through `ob login`, lists your remote vaults, and calls `ob sync-setup` to link the local path. After that the container syncs automatically on restart with no further input.

---

## obsidian-bridge service

Watches the local vault and rebuilds the Quartz static site on change.

1. On startup: copies vault scaffold files to the local vault (new files only), runs initial Quartz build.
2. Watches the vault with chokidar (polling every 5s).
3. On any `.md` change: schedules a debounced Quartz rebuild (3s).

### Quartz build

Quartz v4.4.0 is baked into the Docker image at build time. On each build:
1. `rsync` copies the vault to `quartz-src/content/` (excluding `.obsidian/`)
2. `npx quartz build --output /quartz-output` runs
3. `chmod -R o+r` is applied to the NFS quartz-output so nginx can serve the files

### Vault scaffold

Seed files (templates, research prompt, index pages) live at `machines/dockercompute/docker/services/obsidian-bridge/vault-scaffold/`. The bridge copies them to the local vault on startup (new files only — existing files are never overwritten).

---

## Research Workflow

The stack includes an automated research pipeline. See [RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md](RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md) for full details.

**Quick summary:** In Obsidian, use the Templater **research-request** template to create a new note in `research/inbox/`. Fill it in, then change `status: draft` → `status: pending`. The research-bot picks it up on the next poll (~10 min) and writes structured output into `research/<topic>/`. Changes flow back to your Obsidian clients via obsidian-headless → Obsidian Sync.

---

## Troubleshooting

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `notes.boughtmyown.cloud` 403 | NFS permission bits stripped on quartz-output | `chmod -R o+r /mnt/nfs/MainStoragePool/docker_data/obsidian/quartz-output` |
| Notes site not updating after vault change | Bridge not running or build failed | `docker logs obsidian-bridge --tail 30` |
| Scaffold files missing in Obsidian | Seeding failed on startup | `docker restart obsidian-bridge` |
| Vault changes not reaching Obsidian clients | obsidian-headless not syncing | `docker logs obsidian-headless --tail 30` |
| obsidian-headless waiting for setup | One-time setup not completed | Run `./machines/dockercompute/docker/services/obsidian-headless/setup.sh` |
| obsidian-headless auth expired | Session token stale | `docker exec -it obsidian-headless ob login`, then restart |
| New vault `.md` not triggering rebuild | Chokidar polling lag | Bridge polls every 5s with 3s debounce; wait ~10s |

10	
11	1. **Centralized Management**: All secrets are in one place, making them easier to update and rotate.
12	2. **Consistent Access**: All components (Terraform, Ansible, Docker) access secrets from the same source.
13	3. **Version Control Safety**: The actual secrets file is excluded from Git via `.gitignore`.
14	4. **Documentation**: An example file with placeholders shows what secrets are needed.
15	
16	## Setting Up Secrets
17	
18	### Initial Setup

20	1. Copy the example file to create your secrets file:
21	
22	```bash
23	cp secrets/homelab.env.example secrets/homelab.env
24	```
25	
26	2. Edit the file and fill in your actual secrets:
27

29	nano secrets/homelab.env
30	```
31	
32	3. Secure the file with appropriate permissions:
33	
34	```bash
35	chmod 600 secrets/homelab.env
36	```
37	
38	### Secret Categories
39	
40	The secrets file contains several categories of sensitive information:
41	
42	- **Domain Configuration**: Your domain name
43	- **Proxmox Credentials**: API URL and token for Proxmox access
44	- **VM Credentials**: Username and password for VM access
45	- **Cloudflare Configuration**: API key, email, and zone ID for DNS management
46	- **Service Credentials**: Passwords and tokens for various services
47	
48	## Using Secrets
49	
50	### In Shell Scripts
51	
52	Use the `load_secrets.sh` script to load secrets into your environment:
53	
54	```bash
55	source scripts/load_secrets.sh
56	```
57	
58	This makes all secrets available as environment variables in your shell session.
59	
60	### In Terraform
61	
62	The `apply.sh` script in the Terraform directory automatically loads secrets and creates a temporary tfvars file for use during Terraform operations.
63	
64	### In Docker Compose
65	
66	Docker Compose files reference environment variables that are loaded from the secrets file.
67	
68	### In Ansible
69	
70	Ansible playbooks can access environment variables loaded from the secrets file.
71	
72	## Security Best Practices
73	
74	1. **Never commit secrets**: The `.gitignore` file is configured to exclude the actual secrets file.
75	2. **Use strong passwords**: Generate strong, unique passwords for each service.
76	3. **Rotate secrets regularly**: Update API keys and passwords periodically.
77	4. **Limit access**: Restrict access to the secrets file to authorized users only.
78	5. **Backup securely**: If backing up the secrets file, ensure the backup is also secured.
79	
80	## Adding New Secrets
81	
82	When adding a new service that requires secrets, you must update **four places**:
83	
84	1. **`secrets/homelab.env.example`** — add placeholder with a comment (documents what the var is for).
85	2. **`secrets/homelab.env`** — add the actual value (gitignored; only on your local machine).
86	3. **`machines/dockercompute/docker/deploy-direct.sh`** — add `VAR_NAME=$VAR_NAME` to the `.env` generation block so it gets written into the Docker `.env` file.
87	4. **`.github/workflows/gitops-sync.yml`** — add `VAR_NAME: ${{ secrets.VAR_NAME }}` to both the `validate` job dummy values and the `deploy` job `env:` block. Then add the secret in GitHub → repo Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions.
88	
89	**Rule: Never hardcode secrets in `docker-compose.yml`.** Always use `${VAR_NAME}` references and store the value in `homelab.env` / GitHub Actions secrets. Secrets committed to `docker-compose.yml` end up in git history permanently.
90	
91	## GitHub Actions Secret Passthrough
92	
93	Secrets flow from GitHub Actions to the VM like this:
94	
95	```
96	GitHub Actions secret
97	  → gitops-sync.yml env: block (makes it available in the runner)
98	  → SSH command: export VAR="$VAR" (passes it to the remote shell)
99	  → deploy-direct.sh: writes it to .env file
100	  → docker compose reads .env file
101	  → container environment variable
102	```
103	
104	Note: Secrets passed via `export VAR="..."` in SSH commands are briefly visible in the process table on the VM. This is acceptable for a homelab but worth knowing. The `.env` file is written with `chmod 600`.
105	
106	## Assessment
107	
108	The flat-file + GitHub Actions approach is appropriate for a single-person homelab. It is not meaningfully less secure than managed secrets tools (Doppler, Vault, etc.) for this use case. No migration is needed.
109	
110	## Authelia Secrets
111	
112	Authelia requires three secrets (all passed as environment variables):
113	
114	| Variable | Purpose | Rotation impact |
115	|----------|---------|----------------|
116	| `AUTHELIA_SESSION_SECRET` | Encrypts session cookies | Logs all users out |
117	| `AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY` | Encrypts the SQLite database | Requires DB migration or wipe |
118	| `AUTHELIA_JWT_SECRET` | Signs password reset tokens | Invalidates in-flight reset links |
119	
120	Generate each with:
121	```bash
122	python3 -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_hex(32))"
123	```
124	
125	> **Warning:** Rotating `AUTHELIA_STORAGE_ENCRYPTION_KEY` requires either running `authelia storage encryption change-key` or deleting `services/authelia/db.sqlite3` before restarting. The other two secrets are safe to rotate freely — they just log everyone out.
126	
127	See [AUTHELIA.md](AUTHELIA.md) for full Authelia configuration details.
128	
129	---
130	
131	## Troubleshooting
132	
133	If services fail to start or access APIs, check that:
134	
135	1. The secrets file exists and contains all required values.
136	2. The secrets have been properly loaded into the environment.
137	3. The services are configured to use the correct environment variable names.
138	
139	## Emergency Response
140	
141	If you suspect a secret has been compromised:
142	
143	1. Rotate the affected secret immediately.
144	2. Check for unauthorized access or unusual activity.
145	3. Update the secret in your `homelab.env` file.
146	4. Restart affected services to use the new secret.
